methods.method_name: Ice Core Data Collection and Analysis
methods.description: This method involves collecting and analyzing ice-core data to study radionuclide concentrations and their relationship with climate changes. The data is linked to the GICC05 timescale and includes records from Greenland and Antarctic ice cores.

steps.description: The ice-core 10 Be and 36 Cl data used in this study are shown in Fig. 1.
steps.searchable_summary: fetch ice-core 10 Be and 36 Cl data

steps.description: By calculating fluxes we make a first-order correction for the changing snow accumulation rates between stadials and interstadials and their influence on radionuclide concentrations.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate fluxes to correct for snow accumulation rate changes

steps.description: For each ice core we calculated multiple linear regression models using δ 18 O and snow accumulation rates as predictors for 10 Be (36 Cl) fluxes and subtracted the obtained model from the 10 Be (36 Cl) data.
steps.searchable_summary: perform multiple linear regression to model 10 Be and 36 Cl fluxes

steps.description: To combine all ice-core records, we calculated their mean (denoted as 'stack') using Monte Carlo bootstrapping.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate mean of ice-core records using Monte Carlo bootstrapping

methods.description: This method focuses on collecting and analyzing absolutely dated radiocarbon records to study production rate changes over centennial-to-millennial timescales.

steps.description: For the purpose of this study we have to focus on radiocarbon records that are absolutely dated.
The timescale of the Lake Suigetsu record is based on varve counting, corrected for long-term systematic errors by matching its 14 C record to the 14 C variations in speleothems.
steps.searchable_summary: correct Lake Suigetsu timescale using varve counting and speleothem matching
